facts.fact: Resistance training causes short-term increases in testosterone. Compound movements produce larger acute testosterone responses than isolation exercises.
facts.practical_application: Prioritize compound lifts at 70-85% 1RM at the start of workouts.

facts.fact: Prolonged caloric restriction disrupts testosterone production. Moderate deficits (300-500 kcal) preserve testosterone better than aggressive deficits.
facts.practical_application: Keep deficit at 300-500 kcal max. Include refeed days every 5-7 days.

facts.fact: At BMI >30, body fat is the primary suppressor of testosterone through aromatization. Losing 5-10% body weight can increase testosterone 15-30%.
facts.practical_application: Focus on sustained gradual fat loss rather than testosterone supplements.

facts.fact: Testosterone is primarily produced during REM and deep sleep. Sleeping 5 hours instead of 8 can reduce testosterone by 10-15%. Sleep apnea dramatically suppresses testosterone.
facts.practical_application: Prioritize 7-9 hours quality sleep. Get sleep study if OSA suspected.

facts.fact: Low vitamin D is linked to low testosterone. Supplementation raises testosterone in deficient men but not in those with normal levels.
facts.practical_application: Test 25(OH)D. If below 30 ng/mL, supplement 2000-4000 IU D3 daily.

facts.fact: Zinc supplementation increases testosterone only in zinc-deficient individuals. Extra zinc beyond adequacy does not boost testosterone further.
facts.practical_application: Test before supplementing. 15-30 mg/day max if deficient.

facts.fact: Diets very low in fat (<20% of calories) decrease testosterone. Moderate fat intake of 25-35% of calories is optimal.
facts.practical_application: Include eggs, nuts, ghee, olive oil. Aim for 0.8-1g fat per kg body weight.

facts.fact: Testosterone drops within 30 minutes of alcohol consumption. 3+ drinks suppresses testosterone for 24+ hours. Chronic heavy drinking causes testicular atrophy.
facts.practical_application: Limit to 1-2 drinks, 1-2 times per week max.

facts.fact: Magnesium binds to SHBG, increasing free testosterone bioavailability. 50-60% of adults are magnesium deficient.
facts.practical_application: 200-400 mg magnesium glycinate in the evening.

facts.fact: Cortisol and testosterone operate on a seesaw mechanism. Chronic stress keeps cortisol elevated, continuously suppressing testosterone.
facts.practical_application: Manage stress, sleep, and recovery. Reduce training volume if overtrained.

facts.fact: Chronic sleep disruption is the strongest lifestyle predictor of elevated cortisol. Sleep apnea causes repeated nocturnal cortisol spikes.
facts.practical_application: Treat sleep apnea. Maintain consistent sleep/wake times.

facts.fact: Intense exercise acutely raises cortisol but it drops below baseline hours later. Moderate exercise lowers cortisol. Overtraining keeps cortisol elevated.
facts.practical_application: 150-200 min/week low-to-moderate intensity. Avoid excessive HIIT.

facts.fact: Mindfulness-based stress reduction and deep breathing activate the parasympathetic nervous system, lowering cortisol within minutes.
facts.practical_application: 5-10 min box breathing (4-4-4-4) daily, post-workout or before bed.

facts.fact: Ashwagandha KSM-66 at 300-600mg/day reduces cortisol by 14-28% over 8 weeks. Also reduces stress, anxiety, and may increase testosterone.
facts.practical_application: 300-600mg KSM-66 in evening. 8-12 weeks on, 4 weeks off.

facts.fact: High added sugar intake elevates cortisol and blunts natural stress response.
facts.practical_application: Minimize added sugars.

facts.fact: Dark chocolate (>70% cacao) flavonoids buffer stress reactivity in adrenal glands, lowering cortisol release.
facts.practical_application: 20-30g of 70%+ dark chocolate daily as evening treat.

facts.fact: High omega-3 blood levels are associated with lower cortisol and inflammation. Fish oil 1-3g EPA+DHA may reduce cortisol.
facts.practical_application: 1-2g combined EPA+DHA daily.

facts.fact: Even mild dehydration temporarily increases cortisol. During exercise, dehydration amplifies cortisol response.
facts.practical_application: 3-4 liters water daily at 130 kg body weight.

facts.fact: 70-80% of growth hormone is secreted during Stage 3 deep sleep. Disrupted sleep destroys GH production.
facts.practical_application: Cool room, dark, no screens 60 min before bed, consistent schedule.

facts.fact: Growth hormone increases 2-5x during 12-16 hour fasting. This mobilizes fat for energy.
facts.practical_application: 12-14 hour overnight fast naturally boosts GH.

facts.fact: Exercise above lactate threshold triggers significant GH pulse within 30-60 min post-exercise. Dose-dependent on intensity.
facts.practical_application: Heavy compound lifts at 6-8 RM range trigger GH release.

facts.fact: Arginine and glutamine supplementation have minimal and transient GH effects. Not worth supplementing for GH purposes.
facts.practical_application: Do not waste money on GH boosters. Sleep and exercise are far more effective.

facts.fact: Resistance training dramatically improves insulin sensitivity via GLUT4 translocation. A single session improves sensitivity for 24-48 hours.
facts.practical_application: Consistent resistance training 3x/week.

facts.fact: 10-15 minute post-meal walks reduce postprandial glucose spikes by 20-30%. Contracting muscles pull glucose independent of insulin.
facts.practical_application: Walk 10-15 min after largest meals.

facts.fact: Visceral fat produces inflammatory cytokines causing insulin resistance. Exercise preferentially reduces visceral fat. Visceral fat responds quickly to exercise.
facts.practical_application: Exercise + caloric deficit superior to diet alone for metabolic syndrome.

facts.fact: Replacing refined carbs with complex carbs improves insulin sensitivity even at same total carb intake. Minimum 25-30g fiber/day needed.
facts.practical_application: Choose whole grains, lentils, vegetables over white rice and maida.

facts.fact: A single night of 4-hour sleep reduces insulin sensitivity by 30-50%. Chronic partial sleep deprivation replicates this.
sources: Spiegel et al. sleep-deprivation insulin clamp studies
facts.practical_application: Consistent 7-9 hours quality sleep is non-negotiable for metabolic health.

facts.fact: Within 1-2 weeks of caloric restriction, T3 drops significantly and reverse T3 increases. This is the primary mechanism behind metabolic adaptation.
facts.practical_application: Include refeed days at maintenance every 5-7 days.

facts.fact: Very low carb diets (<50g/day) further suppress T3. Keeping carbs at 150-200g/day during deficit protects thyroid function.
facts.practical_application: Do not go very low carb during recomp. 150-180g/day is optimal.

facts.fact: Reverse dieting (gradually increasing calories by 100-150/week post-cut) may help restore thyroid function and metabolic rate.
facts.practical_application: When reaching goal body fat, add 100-150 cal/week over 4-6 weeks.

facts.fact: Thyroid requires iodine and selenium. Iodized salt generally provides adequate iodine. 2-3 Brazil nuts provide full daily selenium.
facts.practical_application: Use iodized salt. Do not over-supplement iodine.

facts.fact: Alcohol increases cortisol, disrupts sleep architecture (reduces deep sleep 20-40%), decreases GH, impairs protein synthesis 24+ hours, and provides 7 kcal/g of empty calories.
facts.practical_application: Minimize alcohol entirely during active fat loss.

facts.fact: Priority hierarchy for hormone optimization at BMI 40: Tier 1 (sleep study/OSA treatment, resistance training, 300-500 kcal deficit, post-meal walks, 7-9h sleep). Tier 2 (Vitamin D3, Magnesium, Ashwagandha, Fish oil, increase carbs to 150-180g). Avoid: test boosters, arginine, iron, very low carb, aggressive deficits.
facts.practical_application: Follow the tiered protocol.
